title: practical english usage - Part 180
content:
A  relative pronoun is the subject or object of the verb that comes after it. So we There's the girl who works with my sister.

Relative pronouns (who, which and that, ►    233)  also join clauses.

6 relative      pronouns Conjunctions, Sentences and ClausesSection 20 grammar •  231   tense simplification in subordinate clauses I don't care what we have for dinner if I don't have to cook it.

It doesn't matter where we go this summer.

care, I don't mind, it's not important and similar expressions.

Present tenses are also used with future reference after it doesn't matter, I don't I hope you sleep well. I bet he gets married before the end of the year.

I hope (►  490) and I bet (►   401).

In an informal style, present verbs are often used with future meanings after I've got my tennis things in case we have time for a game tomorrow.

main verb is present or past. For details, ►    248.

A  present tense is normally used with a future meaning after in case even if the 5  in   case,     I hope,      I bet,    it  doesn't       matter,        etc For future verbs in (/-clauses (e.g. I'll give you £100  if it will help you to get home), ►    243.

If she rings, I'll tell her that I'll ring back later, (two different future times) I'll hide it somewhere where he'll never find it. (two different future times) I'm sure I won't understand a word of the lecture.

I don't know where she will be tomorrow.

main verb does not refer to the future (or to the same time in the future).

A  future verb is necessary for future reference in a subordinate clause if the 4 future     in  subordinate         clauses:    .. . where       she    will    be not ( At the end of the year there will be an exam on everything you've studied.

I'll phone you when I've finished, (     .. .when J will have finished.) completion.

The  present perfect is used instead of the future perfect, to express the idea of 3   present      perfect:    ... when      I've  finished We'll get there sooner than you do/will.

She'll be on the same train as we are/will tomorrow.

In comparisons with as and than, present and future verbs are both possible.

You can tell who you like next week, but not until then.

Phone me when you arrive. Make sure you come back soon.

This can happen when the main verb is not future in form, but refers to the future.

If she asks what I'm doing in her house, I'll say I'm checking the gas.

He says he'll give five pounds to anybody who finds his pen.

I'll lend it to you on condition that you bring it back tomorrow.

I'll have a good time whether I win or lose. I ll go where you go.

It will be interesting to see whether he recognises you.

Will you stay here until the plane takes off?

I'll think of you when I'm lying on the beach, (     . . .-when-Lwill belying...) not I'll write to her when I have time, (     .. .-wkenlwiU have time.) pronouns, and in indirect speech.

instance after if, whether and on condition that, after question words and relative until, after, before, as soon as, but in most other subordinate clauses - for subordinate clauses. This happens not only after conjunctions of time like when, + infinitive Present tenses are often used instead of will     to refer to the future in 2 present      instead      of  future:    I'll write      when      I have      time.

grammar •  232   subjunctive: that she go, that they be, if I were, etc very common.                                                                                                           -> (e.g. I be, he were). Except for Ilhelshelit were after if, they are not present verbs without -(e)s, (e.g. she see, he have) and special forms of be forms. English only has a few subjunctive forms left: third person singular other modal verbs, by special uses of past tenses (►  46), and by ordinary verb modern English they have mostly been replaced by uses of should, would and are possible, desirable or imaginary. Older English had subjunctives, but in especially in subordinate clauses to talk about 'unreal' situations: things which Some languages have special verb forms called 'subjunctive', which  are used 1  What    is  the   subjunctive?
